journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal of Nursing Studies Advances

title-group.article-title: Patient outcomes of a post-anaesthesia fast-track pathway after outpatient surgery: A systematic review

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Anaesthesia, Intensive Care and Pain Centre, VZW Maria Middelares, Ghent, Belgium
bUniversity Centre for Nursing and Midwifery, Department of Public Health and Primary Care, Ghent University, Ghent, Belgium
cSchool of Health Sciences, Faculty of Medicine and Health, Örebro University, Örebro, Sweden

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author at: Ella Hermie, VZW Maria Middelares, Department of Anaesthesia, Intensive Care and Pain Centre, Buitenring Sint-Denijs 30, 9000 Ghent, Belgium. Ella.Hermie@mijnziekenhuis.be

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

list-item.p: Fast-tracking has been implemented in clinical practice to streamline postoperative patient flows, reduce monitoring demands, and enable earlier discharge.
Prior reviews have primarily focused on anaesthetic and surgical techniques that facilitate fast-tracking, without systematically evaluating its impact on patient outcomes and safety.
Not all patients undergoing elective day surgery require an admission to the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit.
Fast-tracking after outpatient surgery is associated with a shorter postoperative length of stay without increasing readmission rates or postoperative adverse effects.
More high-quality research is needed to assess the effectiveness of fast-tracking and research cost-effectiveness, patient satisfaction, and the specific nursing care needs of fast-tracked patients.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Advances in surgical and anaesthetic techniques have enabled some outpatients to meet the criteria for discharge from the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit while still in the operating room. These patients may benefit from being transferred directly from the operating room to a dedicated recovery area designed to expedite discharge home—a concept known as fast-tracking. Although this innovative approach to postoperative nursing care has already been implemented in clinical practice, its impact on patient outcomes and patient safety remains uncertain.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: This systematic review aims to evaluate the effectiveness of a fast-track pathway compared to the standard recovery pathway after outpatient surgery.

sec.title: Information sources
sec.p: The databases MEDLINE, Embase, Web of Science, CINAHL, Cochrane and Scopus were searched until January 2025.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The review included randomised-controlled trials, case-control studies, prospective and retrospective cohort studies that reported on fast-tracking of adult patients undergoing outpatient surgical procedures. Two reviewers independently assessed the eligibility of the articles identified with the search strategy. The risk of bias was assessed with the Risk of Bias 2 tool for randomised-controlled trials and the Risk of Bias in Non-randomised studies of Interventions for non-randomised controlled trials. Due to the heterogeneity of the included studies, a narrative synthesis was conducted to systematically summarise and interpret the findings across studies.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The literature search identified 5226 unique records, with 7 studies of low quality and high risk of bias included in this review. 5 of the 7 studies were published before 2005. Bypass rates varied from 30 % to 87 %. All included studies reported a shorter postoperative length of stay for the fast-track group compared to the standard recovery pathway. The studies indicated that fast-tracked patients had similar, or in some cases, even better outcomes regarding readmission rates and the incidence of postoperative adverse effects compared to the standard recovery pathway.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Not all patients require admission to the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit after outpatient surgery. Fast-tracking certain patients may offer benefits, such as a shorter postoperative length of stay, without negatively impacting patient outcomes. By reducing length of stay, improving patient flow and optimising resource utilisation, fast-tracking has the potential to enhance the efficiency and organisation of care while maintaining the quality. However, high-quality studies are needed to evaluate the safety and effectiveness of fast-tracking. As fast-tracking combines the care environments of the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit and phase II recovery units, tailored nursing care for fast-track patients may be necessary.

sec.title: Registration
sec.p: CRD4202452974, PROSPERO, Registered 23/03/2024

list-item.p: Fast-tracking has been implemented in clinical practice to streamline postoperative patient flows, reduce monitoring demands, and enable earlier discharge.
Prior reviews have primarily focused on anaesthetic and surgical techniques that facilitate fast-tracking, without systematically evaluating its impact on patient outcomes and safety.
Not all patients undergoing elective day surgery require an admission to the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit.
Fast-tracking after outpatient surgery is associated with a shorter postoperative length of stay without increasing readmission rates or postoperative adverse effects.
More high-quality research is needed to assess the effectiveness of fast-tracking and research cost-effectiveness, patient satisfaction, and the specific nursing care needs of fast-tracked patients.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Over the past few decades, the prevalence of same-day surgery, commonly referred to as ambulatory or outpatient surgery, has rapidly increased in Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development countries (Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development, 2023). Since 2014, more surgeries in Belgium have been performed on an outpatient basis than on an inpatient basis, with a 19.2 % increase in outpatient operations between 2012 and 2019 (Federale Overheidsdienst Volksgezondheid, 2023).

Advances in surgical and anaesthetic techniques have enabled early recovery after outpatient surgery (Orkin, 1996; Song et al., 1998; Watkins and White, 2001; White, 1998). These developments have resulted in patients emerging from anaesthesia comfortably breathing, with stable vital signs, and fully awake when they leave the operating room. They often meet the criteria for discharge from the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit while still in the operating room (Watkins and White, 2001). Traditionally, outpatients are transferred from the operating room to the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit for immediate postoperative recovery from anaesthesia and then to a phase II recovery unit to be prepared for discharge home (standard recovery pathway) (Song, Joshi and White, 1998; Apfelbaum et al., 2002; Joshi and Twersky, 2000). Post-Anaesthetic Care Units are intensive care units with a nurse-to-patient ratio of 1:2. They are associated with high post-operative costs (Apfelbaum et al., 2002; Joshi and Twersky, 2000; Rice et al., 2015), with a 2012 study conducted in France estimating a cost of €10.80 per minute (Raft, Millet and Meistelman, 2015). They primarily focus on patient airway management, haemodynamics, and pain control. In contrast, phase II recovery units prioritize safe discharge, with an emphasis on mobility, patient education, and home health needs (Rice et al., 2021). Therefore, patients who meet discharge criteria while being in the operating room may benefit more from an immediate transfer from the operating room to a designated recovery area designed to assess their home-readiness in preparation for discharge (White, 1998).

The concept of fast-track surgery or enhanced recovery after surgery was introduced to facilitate early discharge from the hospital and a more rapid return to normal activities of daily living after elective surgery (White et al., 2003). Two approaches have emerged since the introduction of fast-tracking in 1996. The first approach is bypassing, where patients are transferred directly from the operating room to a phase II recovery unit, eliminating the need for a traditional Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay (Apfelbaum et al., 2002; Rice et al., 2015; White et al., 2003). However, in situations where the phase II recovery unit is overloaded with patients due to limited space, this strategy may be counterproductive. Therefore, a second approach, the Fast-Track unit, has been developed as an alternative fast-tracking strategy. This approach involves transferring patients from the operating room to a specialised recovery area and afterwards immediately discharging them home. A specialised recovery area differs from a phase II recovery unit, because it is staffed by a single nurse and the nurse-to-patient ratio can vary from 1:2 to 1:4. This area includes beds that allow patients to sit up, chairs for family members, and a mobile unit to monitor blood pressure and oxygen saturation (White et al., 2003).

The Modified Aldrete Criteria and the White-Song Fast-Track Recovery criteria are two commonly used instruments to assess patients’ eligibility for fast-tracking. The Modified Aldrete criteria were originally developed to assess patients’ readiness for discharge from the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit to the phase II recovery unit or home, with a discharge threshold set at a score of ≥ 18 (Aldrete, 1995). Although it is commonly used in the operating room to determine fast-track eligibility, this instrument fails to detect common side effects following surgery such as pain, nausea, and vomiting, which are traditionally treated in the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit. The White-Song Fast-Track recovery criteria refined the Modified Aldrete criteria by including pain and emesis. The maximum score is 14, and patients with a score of ≥ 12 are considered fast-track eligible (White et al., 2003; White and Song, 1999). Another instrument developed to assess patient eligibility for fast-tracking is the Regional Anaesthesia Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit Bypass Criteria. The criteria immediately excludes fast-tracking for patients with hypotension or orthostasis in the operating room. The instrument also excludes patients from bypassing with any nausea, vomiting, pain, shivering, or pruritus.

Understanding the impact of fast-track approaches on patient outcomes is essential for evaluating their effect on patient safety. The findings can help shape the organisation of postoperative nursing care and enhance the quality of care provided to outpatients. While several narrative reviews have discussed best practices for fast-tracking in outpatient surgery (van Vlymen and White, 1998; Watkins and White, 2001; White et al., 2007), to our knowledge, no systematic review has yet assessed the outcomes of fast-tracking after outpatient surgery. This systematic review aims to provide a comprehensive summary of the patient outcomes associated with fast-track pathways and evaluate their effectiveness compared to the standard recovery pathway after outpatient surgery. This review addresses the following research question: What is the effectiveness of a fast-track pathway (intervention) on patient outcomes (outcome) for outpatients (population) in Post-Anaesthesia Care compared to a standard recovery pathway (comparison)?

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-Analyses were used to report this review adhering to the guidelines for systematic reviews from the Cochrane Collaboration (Higgins et al., 2023). The protocol is registered in PROSPERO, the international prospective register of systematic reviews (CRD42024527974).

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: Studies were included in the systematic review, if they met the following eligibility criteria: randomised controlled trials, prospective cohort studies, retrospective cohort studies and case-control studies. Only articles that included a population of adults, aged 18 years or older were included, as paediatric patients need to be separated from adult patients in the postoperative pathway. Fast-track surgery or enhanced recovery after surgery is a multimodal perioperative care pathway that aims to facilitate early recovery after surgical procedures. The concept is used in both inpatient and outpatient surgery. Because this review focused on patients undergoing outpatient surgical procedures, only articles on outpatients were included. Outpatients are defined as patients who are admitted for a surgical procedure, undergo the operation and are discharged on the same calendar day, without the need for an overnight stay. The intervention in the included studies had to be fast-tracking, also known as bypassing or a fast-track unit. The comparison was a standard recovery pathway with a traditional stay in the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit after surgery followed by a transfer to a phase II recovery unit. Due to the language skills of the reviewers, this review only included articles reported in English, French, Dutch or German. There were no restrictions set on participant characteristics, publication date or geographical regions.

sec.title: Information sources
sec.p: A search of the databases MEDLINE, Embase, Web of Science, CINAHL, Cochrane, and Scopus was conducted to identify literature published until January 2025. An email alert was set up to notify the first reviewer (EH) when new articles were published during the review process. Additionally, Clinicaltrials.gov was searched for unpublished and ongoing trials. Articles were retrieved electronically and imported into Endnote.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: A librarian was consulted for advice and to review the search strategy. The strategy involved key terms combined with Boolean operators. Terms were defined using the Population, Intervention, Comparison, and Outcome (PICO)-framework and included "outpatient surgery", "fast-tracking", and "Post-Anaesthetic Care (Unit)". The Boolean operator "OR" was used to combine terms within the same concept, while "AND" was used to link different concepts. The search strategy was customized for each database, incorporating both index terms and free-text words where applicable. No limits were applied when searching the databases. The search strategy for each database is provided in Supplementary material A.

sec.title: Selection process
sec.p: Two reviewers (EH, AVD) independently assessed the eligibility of the titles and abstracts identified by the defined search strategy. A Cohen’s kappa of 0.54 indicated a moderate inter-rater agreement between the two reviewers. The reviewers reached consensus on articles where there was disagreement or uncertainty through discussion. The full-text of the articles that passed the initial screening were read. The same reviewers closely examined the full-text of these articles and assessed their eligibility based on pre-established inclusion and exclusion criteria. Backward and forward citation searches using Web of Science core collection were used to identify other relevant articles.

sec.title: Data collection process
sec.p: The first reviewer (EH) extracted the data using a structured data extraction form created in Excel, to collect relevant information to answer the research question. A second reviewer (AVD) verified the extracted data.

sec.title: Data items
sec.p: The evidence table included information on the study characteristics, patient characteristics, intervention details, patient outcomes, and study results.

sec.title: Assessment of risk of bias
sec.p: The quality and risk of bias was evaluated by two independent reviewers (EH, HH). For randomised controlled trials, the Risk of Bias 2 tool was used (Sterne et al., 2019), while the Risk Of Bias In Non-randomised Studies - of Interventions, Version 2-tool was used for case-control, prospective, and retrospective cohort studies (Sterne et al., 2016). The reviewers reached consensus through discussion on the domains where there was disagreement.

sec.title: Synthesis methods
sec.p: A meta-analysis was not feasible due to the heterogeneity in population, methodology, outcome definitions, and data availability across the included studies. Consequently, a narrative synthesis was conducted to systematically summarize and interpret the results.

sec.title: Certainty assessment
sec.p: The Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation was used to assess the body of evidence. The following domains were evaluated to assess the confidence in the evidence found: risk of bias, inconsistency, indirectness, imprecision and publication bias (Higgins et al., 2023). Due to the heterogeneity of the included outcomes, it was not feasible to pool data across studies to generate a single effect estimate. Instead, a narrative summary of the effect across the studies was conducted (Murad et al., 2017).

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p:
The literature search identified 8214 records. After removing duplicates, 5226 records remained. Title and abstract screening identified 44 records eligible for full-text review. Full-text articles were unavailable for 12 reports. A total of 24 articles were excluded for the following reasons: incorrect publication type (n = 17), irrelevant intervention (n = 5), predetermined key outcomes not reported (n = 2), or incorrect definition of outpatient surgery (n = 1). Seven studies were ultimately included in this review. Backward and forward citation searches did not uncover any additional studies that met the eligibility criteria. The PRISMA flowchart is shown in Fig. 1.Fig. 1
PRISMA FLOW diagram.Fig. 1

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p:
The following study designs were included: prospective observational studies (n = 4) (Apfelbaum et al., 2002; Duncan et al., 2001; Rice et al., 2021; Rice et al., 2015), retrospective observational studies (n = 2) (White et al., 2003; Williams et al., 2002), and a prospective randomised controlled trial (n = 1) (Song et al., 2004). Studies were conducted in the United States of America (n = 3) (Apfelbaum et al., 2002; Williams et al., 2002; White et al., 2003), the United Kingdom (n = 2) (Rice et al., 2021; Rice et al., 2015), and Canada (n = 2) (Song et al., 2004; Duncan et al., 2001). The studies were either multicentric (n = 2) (Apfelbaum et al., 2002; Duncan et al., 2001) or monocentric (n = 5). The studies were conducted in a variety of settings: community-based hospitals (n = 5), surgical centres (n = 2), and university hospitals (n = 3). One study did not specify the setting (Williams et al., 2002). Sample sizes ranged from 150 to 2354 outpatients. Five studies were published before 2005 and two studies after 2015 (Rice et al., 2021; Rice et al., 2015). A summary of the included studies is provided in Table 1.Table 1
Summary of the included studies.Table 1:

sec.title: Risk of bias in studies
sec.p:
The risk of bias assessment with the Risk Of Bias In Non-randomised Studies - of Interventions, Version 2 is shown in Fig. 2 and with the Risk of Bias 2 in Fig. 3. Six studies had an overall serious risk of bias (Apfelbaum et al., 2002; Rice et al., 2021; Rice et al., 2015) and one study had a critical risk of bias (White et al., 2003). All studies assessed with the Risk Of Bias In Non-randomised Studies - of Interventions, Version 2 had a serious risk of bias due to confounding, and all studies had a low risk of bias in classification of interventions. Six out of seven studies had a serious risk of bias in the selection of the reported results.Fig. 2
Risk of bias summary: author's assessment about each methodological quality item for each included study using Risk Of Bias In Non-randomised Studies - of Interventions, Version 2.Fig 2Fig. 3
Risk of bias summary: author's assessment about each methodological quality item for each included study using Risk Of Bias 2.Fig 3

Average nausea score (11-point rating scale) in hospital [mean(range)]0(0–3.1)0(0–1.0)No information
Nausea: Need for antiemetics in hospital [N( %)]10(9.1)8(8.2)18(8.7)
Nausea: Incidence of nausea at home (until 24 h postoperative) [N( %)]4(3.6)5(5.2)9(4.3)†
p < 0.05⁎
p < 0.001, SD = Standard Deviation, CI = Confidence Interval.

sec.title: Patient characteristics
sec.p: Two studies did not report information on patient characteristics such as age, sex or American Society of Anesthesiologists physical status (Duncan et al., 2001; White et al., 2003). Only three studies compared patient characteristics between the fast-track group and the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group. In these studies, the mean age of patients in the fast-track group was lower than that of patients in the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group (Rice et al., 2021; Rice et al., 2015; Song et al., 2004), with one study showing a statistically significant difference (p < 0.001) (Rice et al., 2021). No statistically significant differences in fast-tracking by sex were found (Rice et al., 2021; Rice et al., 2015; Song et al., 2004). Two studies only included patients with American Society of Anesthesiologists physical status of I or II (Duncan et al., 2001; Song et al., 2004) and one study included patients with American Society of Anesthesiologists physical status between I and III (Apfelbaum et al., 2002). Two studies did not restrict enrolment based on American Society of Anesthesiologists physical status (Rice et al., 2021; Rice et al., 2015). Patients with American Society of Anesthesiologists physical status I to IV were fast-tracked (Rice et al., 2021; Rice et al., 2015; Song et al., 2004; Williams et al., 2002).

sec.title: Surgical procedures
sec.p: Patients undergoing various surgical procedures were fast-tracked. Two studies focused exclusively on fast-tracking after orthopaedic surgery (Duncan et al., 2001; Williams et al., 2002), while the other studies implemented fast-tracking for all outpatient procedures. All studies included orthopaedic surgery, with other common procedures being ophthalmic, gynaecologic, urologic, otolaryngologic and gastrointestinal surgery.

sec.title: Anaesthesia
sec.p: Two studies used a standardised anaesthesia protocol for all enrolled patients, meaning all patients received the same drugs and inhalation agents (Duncan et al., 2001; Song et al., 2004), while the other studies allowed different types of anaesthesia to be used. All types of anaesthesia were used in these studies, including general anaesthesia, monitored anaesthesia care and local anaesthesia, and patients could be fast-tracked for all types of anaesthesia.

sec.title: Intervention
sec.p: Five of the included studies compared Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit bypassing with a regular Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay, where patients were transferred to the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit and then to the phase II recovery unit. In two of the included studies, the intervention was a fast-track unit (White et al., 2003; Rice et al., 2021).

All of the included studies used fast-track criteria to determine whether a patient could be fast-tracked or had to be transferred to the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit. The White-Song fast-track recovery criteria were used to assess fast-track eligibility in five of the included articles. One study used the Regional Anaesthesia Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit Bypass Criteria (Williams et al., 2002). While another study used their institutional recovery discharge criteria to assess fast-track eligibility. The patient had to meet all criteria and, according to the anaesthetist’s judgement, be suitable for transfer to the phase II recovery unit (Apfelbaum et al., 2002).

sec.title: Bypass rates
sec.p: All studies reported Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit bypass rates, the number of patients who met the fast-track criteria and were therefore successfully fast-tracked, as a proportion of the total number of patients assessed for eligibility. Bypass rates ranged from 30 % to 87 %.

sec.title: Length of stay
sec.p: All included studies reported a shorter postoperative length of stay, from arrival in the recovery area until discharge home, in the fast-track group compared to the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group. Length of stay in the fast-track group was between 12 and 90 min shorter than in the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group. Five of the included studies reported a statistically significant shorter postoperative stay in the fast-track group compared to the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group. The other two studies did not assess the significance of this difference nor reported p-values (Duncan et al., 2001; White et al., 2003). Only one study reported the total length of stay, from arrival at the facility until discharge home, which was statistically significantly shorter in the fast-track group compared to the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group (121±42.8 min versus 160±36.7 min; p < 0.05) (Duncan et al., 2001).

sec.title: Postoperative adverse effects
sec.p: Five of the included studies reported on the occurrence of postoperative adverse effects (Apfelbaum et al., 2002; Duncan et al., 2001; Song et al., 2004; White et al., 2003; Williams et al., 2002). Two studies reported on postoperative pain (Apfelbaum et al., 2002; Song et al., 2004) and two studies on the need for postoperative analgesia (Duncan et al., 2001; Song et al., 2004). Postoperative nausea was reported in three studies (Apfelbaum et al., 2002; Duncan et al., 2001; Song et al., 2004), and one study also reported on postoperative drowsiness and inability to void (Apfelbaum et al., 2002).

Williams et al. (2002) found that statistically significantly more patients in the fast-track group (30.5 %) compared to the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group (15.5 %) (p < 0.001) required at least one nursing intervention in the phase II recovery unit for common postoperative side effects such as somnolence, shivering, pruritis, pain, urinary retention, and postoperative nausea and vomiting. Furthermore, White et al. (2003) reported no post-discharge anaesthesia or surgery-related side effects requiring a therapeutic intervention.

One study reported statistically significantly more inadequate pain control in the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group (9.8 %) compared with the fast-track group (3.1 %) (p < 0.05) (Apfelbaum et al., 2002). However, another study found no statistically significant difference in in-hospital pain scores, measured using the 11-point numerical rating scale (Song et al., 2004).

Fewer patients in the fast-track group (14.6 %) required analgesics stronger than acetaminophen, codeine 30 mg, than in the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group (36.1 %), although the study did not specify whether this difference was statistically significant (Duncan et al., 2001). Another study found no statistical significance in the number of patients requiring in-hospital analgesia between the fast-track group (30.0 %) and the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group (30.9 %) as well as at home, with 56.4 % and 56.7 % of the patients, respectively (Song et al., 2004).

Two studies found no statistically significant difference in postoperative nausea between the two groups (Duncan et al., 2001; Song et al., 2004). However, one study reported statistically significantly less patients with postoperative nausea in the fast-track group (1.8 %) compared with the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group (6.6 %) (p < 0.05) (Apfelbaum et al., 2002).

One study found that statistically significantly less patients experienced postoperative drowsiness in the fast-track group (1.9 %) compared with the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group (4.0 %) (p < 0.05) (Apfelbaum et al., 2002).

In one study, the incidence of inability to void was statistically significantly lower in the fast-track group (0.4 %) compared with the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group (1.7 %) (p < 0.05) (Apfelbaum et al., 2002).

sec.title: Readmission rates
sec.p: Three of the included studies reported readmission rates. One study reported no readmissions in the fast-track group, but no information was given on the readmission rates in the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group (White et al., 2003). Two studies reported a statistically significantly (p < 0.001) lower readmission rate in the fast-track group compared with the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group (Apfelbaum et al., 2002; Williams et al., 2002). In one study, of the 24 patients readmitted, the reasons for readmission were: pain (n = 3), postoperative nausea and vomiting (n = 6), both pain and postoperative nausea and vomiting (n = 1), somnolence (n = 10), comorbidities (n = 1), and clinical pathway deviation from standing orders for management of postoperative nausea and vomiting and pain (n = 3) (Williams et al., 2002).

sec.title: Reasons for not fast-tracking
sec.p: Two studies reported on the reasons for failed fast-tracking, including medical reasons (Duncan et al., 2001; White et al., 2003), patient-related factors, administrative factors (White et al., 2003), failure to achieve Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit discharge criteria, and change of the planned surgical procedure (Duncan et al., 2001).

sec.title: Costs
sec.p: Three of the included studies calculated the cost savings associated with a fast-track procedure. Rice et al. (2015) calculated a cost saving of $606.99 per fast-track eligible patient. Song et al. (2004) found a statistically significant cost saving of C$50 per arthroscopic surgical procedure (p < 0.05). However, the cost savings were not statistically significant for hysteroscopic (C$10) and laparoscopic (C$6) procedures. Duncan et al. (2001) reported drug costs of $20.58 per fast-track case compared with $14.17 for the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group. However, the calculated cost was $18.84 if the recommended protocol for fast-tracked cases would be strictly adhered to. This suggests that further cost savings could be made.

sec.title: Patient satisfaction
sec.p: One study included patient satisfaction and experience as an outcome. Song et al. (2004) found no statistically significant difference in the satisfaction with recovery between both groups. Rice et al. (2021) reported an improvement in the hospital Press Ganey scores for outpatients, with the score increasing from 91 % to 92 %.

sec.title: Nursing care
sec.p: One study found that the time from the end of anaesthesia to discharge home was statistically significantly shorter in the fast-track group. However, the two groups had similar overall patient care hours and nursing interventions, and therefore there was no decrease in the total nursing workload in the postoperative care units (Song et al., 2004). Williams et al. (2002) found that statistically significantly more patients in the fast-track group (30.5 %) compared with the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group (15.6 %) (p < 0.001) required at least one nursing intervention in the phase II recovery unit for common postoperative adverse effects such as pain and postoperative nausea and vomiting.

One study assessed the inter-rater reliability of patients’ eligibility scores for fast-tracking between anaesthetists and nurses. They agreed that patients met eligibility for fast-tracking in 98 % of the cases (kappa value of 0.966) (Rice et al., 2015).

sec.title: Certainty of evidence
sec.p: The Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation approach was used to narratively summarize the evidence for the effect of fast-tracking. The results are presented in Online Supplemental Appendix B. Online Supplemental Appendix C summarizes the certainty of the evidence, reflecting the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation judgement across different domains.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Not all patients undergoing elective day surgery require an admission to the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit. Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit fast-tracking has been associated with improved efficiency and patient care (White et al., 2003), by streamlining postoperative patient flows without compromising safety or satisfaction (Apfelbaum et al., 2002; Duncan et al., 2001; Song et al., 2004; White et al., 2003). This approach facilitates earlier discharge home, reduces monitoring requirements and paperwork (White, 1998), and optimises resource utilisation (Twersky, Sapozhnikova and Toure, 2008), potentially leading to a reduction in the overall cost of recovery care (White, 1998). In addition, this strategy is associated with a reduction in the time patients are at risk of airway obstruction and haemodynamic instability (Watkins and White, 2001). Many studies have investigated the best practices for fast-tracking after outpatient surgery, focusing on anaesthesia and surgical techniques that facilitate fast-tracking (Fredman et al., 2002; Ali Hassan, 2015; Corey, Bulka and Ehrenfeld, 2014). In most studies, fast-tracking is considered an outcome rather than the intervention itself, leading to uncertainty about its impact on patient outcomes and safety. Therefore, this systematic review aimed to provide a comprehensive overview of patient outcomes associated with a fast-track pathway and to assess its effectiveness compared with a regular Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay after outpatient surgery.

Information on the patient characteristics of the fast-tracked patients remains limited. Only three studies compared the characteristics of the fast-tracked group with those of the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group (Rice et al., 2021; Rice et al., 2015; Song et al., 2004). Two studies found no statistically significant differences in age, sex and American Society of Anesthesiologists physical status between the two groups (Rice et al., 2015; Song et al., 2004). Rice et al. (2021) found statistically significant differences in age and American Society of Anesthesiologists physical status between the fast-track group and Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group. However, it is important to note that they included both inpatients and outpatients and did not differentiate patient characteristics between these groups. In their study, patients with an American Society of Anesthesiologists physical status score of IV were fast-tracked, but it is uncertain whether these are inpatients and/or outpatients (Rice et al., 2021). Whether a patient can be fast-tracked or not, cannot be determined solely based on patient characteristics, surgical procedure or anaesthesia technique.

The included studies showed that not all patients undergoing elective day surgery require the intensive care and monitoring provided in a Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit. Between 30 % and 87 % of patients were immediately transferred to the phase II recovery unit or fast-track unit after outpatient surgery (Song et al., 2004; Rice et al., 2015; Rice et al., 2021). Although all studies used fast-track criteria to determine whether a patient could be fast-tracked, in some studies, patients were transferred to the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit because of the anaesthetist’s preference (Rice et al., 2021). This suggests that there is potential to improve Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit bypass rates.

Recovery is an ongoing process that begins at the end of intraoperative care and continues until the patient has returned to their preoperative physiological state. The recovery process is divided into three phases: (1) Early recovery: From the cessation of anaesthetic agents to the recovery of protective reflexes and motor function. This phase usually takes place in the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit, with close monitoring and supervision. (2) Intermediate recovery: Immediate clinical recovery, is the phase in which patient’s vital signs, orientation, and coordination, and fine motor skills return to normal, and the patient meets the criteria for discharge home, and indicating readiness to return home. (3) Late recovery, which occurs when the patient has fully recovered physically and psychologically and has returned to the preoperative physiological state. This phase can last from a few hours up to several days after surgery (Awad and Chung, 2006; Fang, Wang, Xu, 2023; McGrath and Chung, 2003). In all included studies, patients in the fast-track group had a shorter average postoperative length of stay, between 12 and 90 min, than those in the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit group. This shorter stay can be explained by a reduction in transfer time; patients do not need to be transferred from the operating room to the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit and then to the phase II recovery unit, but are transferred directly from the operating room to the phase II recovery unit or fast-track unit. In some cases, patients are held in the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit, despite meeting discharge criteria, because of overcrowding in the phase II recovery unit. Fast-tracking allows patients to start the first two phases of recovery in the fast-track unit or the phase II recovery unit, allowing them to return home, and to start the final phase of the recovery process in their home environment sooner. Although there are advantages to shorter postoperative recovery stays, there are also potential disadvantages. These include concerns associated with early discharge such as pain control, mobilisation, and identification of postoperative adverse effects (Sibbern et al., 2017).

The results in terms of readmission rates and the occurrence of common postoperative adverse effects such as pain and nausea were comparable between the fast-track and Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group (White et al., 2003; Apfelbaum et al., 2002; Song et al., 2004). In the study by Apfelbaum et al. (2002), the fast-track group even had statistically significantly better outcomes. This may suggest that fast-tracking is not only a safe postoperative alternative but may also have benefits for patients. However, this should be interpreted with caution, as it is based on one study with a serious risk of bias.

Only two of the included studies assessed patient satisfaction (Rice et al., 2021; Song et al., 2004). One study assessed patient satisfaction with recovery using a categorical outcome variable, with response options of excellent, good, fair, or poor (Song et al., 2004). The other study reported an increase in the hospital Press Ganey scores for outpatients in their discussion (Rice et al., 2021). However, details are lacking, raising questions about the patients included in this survey, whether it was limited to fast-track patients or included all outpatients. The limited research on this topic makes it impossible to draw definitive conclusions about patient satisfaction with fast-tracking.

Three studies have evaluated the cost implications of fast-tracking, but reported savings per patient vary considerably. Rice et al. (2015) estimated a cost saving of $606,99 per fast-tracked patient, while Song et al. (2004) found a statistically significant cost saving of C$50 (equivalent to $38 in 2004) for arthroscopic surgery only. No statistically significant cost savings were found for laparoscopic and hysteroscopic procedures. Duncan et al. (2001) focused solely on drug costs, with higher drug costs for fast-tracked cases. Other potential cost savings from fast-tracking patients were not evaluated in this study. Since then, other studies suggest that the increased pharmaceutical costs associated with fast-track anaesthesia may be balanced by reductions in nursing labour costs, particularly when Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit recovery time is shortened (Dexter, Epstein and Marian, 2023; Eberhart et al., 2004). Cost savings remain inherently heterogeneous due to institutional differences such as staffing models, making it difficult to draw general conclusions about the overall financial impact of fast-tracking. Earlier studies have shown that bypassing the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit does not directly lead to net reductions in facility or health system costs (Dexter et al., 2005; Dexter et al., 1999; Dexter and Tinker, 1995), mainly because of the organisation of nursing staffing. Instead, time savings offer a more consistent and generalisable metric, particularly reductions in recovery time. This is supported by the log-normal distribution of Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit recovery times, a pattern recognized for over two decades. While bypass rates alone are insufficient to reduce healthcare costs, decreasing the mean Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit time can lower nursing labour expenses, reduce the total time until the last patient exits the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit, and minimize the incidence of patients recovering in operating rooms due to unavailable Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit staff or beds (Dexter, Epstein and Marian, 2023). Achieving time reductions across a broad patient population is crucial for reducing the length of stay and realizing net cost savings (Dexter and Wachtel, 2014). Therefore, the true value of fast-tracking may not lie in bypassing the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit itself, but in achieving meaningful reductions in recovery time (Dexter, Epstein and Marian, 2023).

Fast-tracking after outpatient surgery is not limited to the adult working population. Studies have shown that children and geriatric patients can also be successfully fast-tracked. Patel et al. (2001) found that paediatric patients in the fast-track group required less pain medication during the recovery phase (40 % in the fast-track group versus 60 % in the control group). In addition, the postoperative length of stay was statistically significant shorter in the fast-track group compared to the control group (99.4 ± 48.6 min versus 79.1 ± 48.3 min; p = 0.008). Fredman et al. (2002) showed that geriatric patients (over 65 years of age) can safely bypass the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit after urological surgery. As mentioned before, Rice et al. (2021) included both inpatients and outpatients. The results showed that inpatients who were fast-tracked spent an average of 49 min less in the recovery area than those who were not fast-tracked (p = 0.001).

Williams et al. (2002) found that more patients in the intervention group compared with the control group required at least one nursing intervention in the phase II recovery unit. It is important to note that the number of interventions performed in the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit for patients in the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group was not included. For the fast-track group, this covers the entire postoperative period, whereas for the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit stay group, it only covers their time in the phase II recovery unit. Song et al. (2004) suggest that although the total number of nursing interventions and nursing hours did not differ between the two recovery groups, the shorter discharge time observed in the fast-track group can be attributed to the nature of post-operative monitoring in the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit. Specifically, the additional monitoring provided during the early recovery phase represents only a small fraction of the overall nursing workload. The majority of nursing activities, such as intravenous therapy, medication administration, hygiene care, patient education, ambulation, and emotional support, constitute the bulk of nursing input.

While most studies have investigated the best practices for fast-tracking, focusing on anaesthesia and surgical techniques, there has been little research into the nursing care involved in this process. Fast-tracking combines the care environments of the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit and the phase II recovery unit, influencing the nature of the nursing care provided. Phase II recovery nurses are less familiar with the immediate post-anaesthesia and postoperative care that needs to be delivered, even though the first hours after surgery are critical for the early detection of complications. Tailored preoperative and operative nursing care also needs to be provided. Currently, there is little to no scientific evidence available regarding what this adapted nursing care should entail.

As expected, there was heterogeneity in the populations included in the studies making it difficult to generalise the results of this systematic review to all outpatients, as it overlooks the complexity of care and the individual patient. However, all studies were conducted in high-income countries and included patients undergoing orthopaedic surgery with a low American Society of Anesthesiologists physical status (I to III). Therefore, these patients are most likely to benefit from Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit fast-tracking.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: Despite comprehensive search strategies, it is possible that relevant studies were missed. The number of studies included in this systematic review is limited, which may be because fast-tracking after outpatient surgery is a common practice. In Japan, most hospitals do not have a Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit, so the concept known as fast-tracking is the standard clinical practice there (Sento et al., 2017; Thenuwara et al., 2018). A survey of 155 Japanese hospitals between October 2012 and May 2015 found that only 16.1 % had a Post-Anaesthetic Care unit (Sento et al., 2017). Therefore, evaluations are likely to be conducted within healthcare institutions and may not be published in peer-reviewed literature.

Evidence on the effectiveness of a fast-track pathway, is limited by the few available studies, the outdated literature on the topic, and the low quality with a high risk of bias of the individual studies included in this systematic review. A meta-analysis was not feasible due to the heterogeneity of the reported outcomes and the different methods used to measure the outcomes.

The included studies were of low quality and had a serious or critical risk of bias. This is likely due to the inclusion of older articles in this systematic review. Some studies were published before the introduction of clinical trials databases, such as clinicaltrials.gov, which aim to enhance transparency in trials and analyses. Additionally, reporting guidelines such as the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology guidelines for observational studies and Consolidated Standards of Reporting Trials guidelines for randomised controlled trials were introduced in 2004 and 2001, respectively, and may not have been common practice at the time. Therefore, some of the included studies lack sufficient information to assess the quality, resulting in a low-quality rating. Given the limited number of available studies, none were excluded based on quality. Therefore, it is crucial to be cautious when interpreting the results of the included studies.

sec.title: Future research
sec.p: Future research is needed to evaluate the effectiveness of fast-tracking as a safe and viable postoperative alternative for outpatients. To reduce heterogeneity and enable meaningful meta-analysis, future studies should employ rigorous methodologies and standardized outcome measures. In particular, more data are needed on both short- and long-term patient outcomes, incorporating not only clinical endpoints but also patient-reported outcome measures and patient-reported experience measures. Additionally, qualitative studies exploring the experiences and perceptions of both patients and healthcare providers can offer valuable insight into the practical implementation and acceptability of fast-tracking pathways. Another important area for further investigation is the development of tailored nursing care for fast-track patients.

Uncertainty remains regarding the cost-effectiveness of fast-tracking, warranting further economic evaluations. Identifying which patients are most suitable for fast-tracking is another important research priority, as this could support the development of evidence-based eligibility criteria. Moreover, it may be valuable to explore alternative fast-tracking models—such as a short-stay Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit that delivers intensive, focused recovery care and discharges patients directly home.

By reducing length of stay, improving patient flow and optimizing resource utilisation, Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit fast-tracking has the potential to enhance the efficiency and organisation of care while maintaining the quality of care provided to outpatients. Future studies could investigate the impact of fast-tracking on staff workload and workflow efficiency, as well as its implications for hospital resource utilisation. Finally, implementation science approaches could help identify barriers and facilitators to adopting fast-tracking protocols across different healthcare settings, ensuring safe and effective integration into routine practice.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Not all patients need to be admitted to the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit after outpatient surgery. Fast-tracking might have benefits for patients such as a shorter length of stay without adversely affecting outcomes, and in some studies outcomes even improved. Patients in the fast-track group did not seem to have higher readmission rates or more postoperative adverse effects than those who stayed in the Post-Anaesthetic Care Unit after outpatient surgery. However, high-quality studies are needed to assess the effectiveness of fast-tracking as a safe postoperative alternative for outpatients.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This research did not receive any specific grant from any funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors. This trial was supported by the Department of Anaesthesia, Intensive Care and Pain Medicine of the General Hospital Maria Middelares, Ghent, Belgium.

sec.title: Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
sec.p: During the preparation of this work the authors used Microsoft Copilot and DeepL Write in order to improve readability and language. After using this tool/service, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and takes full responsibility for the content of the publication.

sec.title: Registration and protocol
sec.p: Prospero registration number: CRD4202452974.

sec.title: Availability of data, code and other materials
sec.p: The data extraction form and extracted data are available upon request.

sec.title: Disclaimers
sec.p: The views expressed in this article are solely those of the authors and do not necessarily reflect the views of their institutions.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Ella Hermie: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Nicky Van Der Vekens: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Hannelore Hofman: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Annelies Van Damme: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Alexander Verheggen: Writing – review & editing. Ann Van Hecke: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Dimitri Beeckman: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: None.

fn.p: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ijnsa.2025.100458.
